sections.paragraph:
Σαμάρεια, ας, ἡ (to be spelled and accented on the analogy of Ἀλεξάνδρεια etc.; s. B-D-F §38, but also Rob. 197; Mlt-H. 147) Samaria (the Gk. form of the name in Polyb. 5, 71, 11; Strabo 16, 2, 34; Diod S 19, 93, 7; Pliny 5, 13, 17; LXX; ParJer 8:11; AscIs; Joseph. index), orig. the name of the city (Hebr. שֹׁמְרוֹן, Aram. שָׁמְרַיִן), though not so in our lit., then transferred to the whole province which, in NT times, included the region fr. the Plain of Jezreel southward to the border of Judea. J 4:4 (Jos., Vi. 269 ἔδει διʼ ἐκείνης [=τ. Σαμαρείας] πορεύεσθαι, Ant. 20, 118); 4:5; Ac 8:9, 14. W. Judea 1:8; 8:1; w. Judea and Galilee 9:31; w. Galilee Lk 17:11; w. Phoenicia Ac 15:3. γυνὴ ἐκ τῆς Σαμαρείας a Samaritan woman J 4:7. ἡ πόλις τῆς Σαμαρείας the (main) city of Samaria (s. πόλις 1), i.e. the city of Samaria which, since the time of Herod the Great, was known as Sebaste Ac 8:5 (on this s. JBoehmer, ZNW 9, 1908, 216–18).—GHölscher, Palästina in der persischen u. hellenist. Zeit 1903; Baedeker, Palästina u. Syrien7 1910; HGuthe, RE XVII 1906, 419ff; XXIV 1913, 448f; Schürer index, s. esp. II 16f n. 50; LHaefeli, Gesch. der Landschaft Sam. von 722 b.c. bis 67 a.d. 1922; AParrot, Samaria, trans. SHooke, ’58.—Kl. Pauly IV 1529f; BHHW III 1655–60; OEANE II 463–67; TRE XXIX, 744–50.—M-M. DBS XI, 740–56. TW.

B-D-F B-D-F = FBlass, ADebrunner, RFunk, A Greek Grammar of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature—List 6

Rob. Rob. = ATRobertson, A Grammar of the Greek NT in the Light of Historical Research—List 6

Mlt-H. Mlt-H. = JMoulton/WHoward, A Grammar of NT Greek II: Accidence and Word-formation—List 6

Gk. Gk. = Greek

Polyb Polyb , III–II b.c.—List 5

Strabo Strabo , I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Diod S Diod S , I b.c.—List 5

LXX LXX = Septuaginta, ed. ARahlfs, unless otherwise specified—Lists 2, beg.

ParJer ParJer = Paraleipomena Jeremiou, II? a.d.—List 2

AscIs AscIs = Ascension of Isaiah—List 2

Joseph Joseph This abbr. used when no specific texts are cited (s. Jos.); I a.d.—List 5

orig. orig. = original(ly)

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

NT NT = New Testament

  1. fr. = from

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

i.e.

i.e. = id est (that is)

I.-E. Indo-European

ZNW ZNW = Zeitschrift für die ntl. Wissenschaft—List 6

Baedeker Baedeker = KB., Palästina u. Syrien—List 6

RE RE = Realencyclopädie für protestantische Theologie und Kirche—List 6

esp. esp. = especially

trans. trans. = transitive

Kl. Pauly Kl. Pauly = Der Kleine Pauly: Lexikon der Antike—List 6

BHHW BHHW = Biblisch-historisches Handwörterbuch—List 6

OEANE OEANE = Oxford Encyclopedia of Archaeology in the Near East—List 6

TRE TRE = Theologische Realenzyklopädie—List 6

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

DBS DBS = Dictionnaire de la Bible, Supplements—List 6

TW TW = Theologisches Wörterbuch zum NT; tr. GBromiley, Theological Dictionary of the NT—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 912
